title: Lip Injection Tutorial
excerpt: Have you ever finished a lip treatment, showed your patient the mirror, and then just thought, "Yikes, it doesn't look that good. Is it my fault? What have I done incorrectly?" You are not alone in the situation. We all feel it.

segments.text: Have you ever finished a lip treatment, showed your patient the mirror, and then just thought, "Yikes, it doesn't look that good. Is it my fault? What have I done incorrectly?" You are not alone in the situation. We all feel it. Every injector has mirror anxiety if they care about their patients. We all want them to be delighted. And sometimes what you see is not the ideal that you're aiming for. In this video, I'm going to show you the lip assessment that I use to stop guessing and start treating with confidence. If you're going to say the lip lacks one thing, do you actually have the injection that will solve that problem? Every attribute of the lip that you assess should have a technique that will help improve it. When you can see the link between what you see and what you can do to improve it, then you become much more confident in the whole process. In this video, I'm going to show you an assessment of a patient where we broke down the components of the lip. the big picture stuff like proportion and the smaller picture but very important stuff such as the details, the angles, the volume in the lips and how we use that to create a

treatment plan to deliver the result that she wanted. What are the key components when assessing lips? The first thing would be proportion. That means the width of the mouth in context of the rest of the face. We want a ratio, golden ratio of intercantal distance to the lips and the chin should be the same. in a female. These are all the same width and the lips project out at the golden ratio. The golden ratio is also evident in many people in terms of the positioning of the mouth. So the position of your chin relative to your filtrum is also on the ratio. And then we have projection. How far forward do the lips project? Now I find this one, this is Steiner's line, projection is the least consistent because noses and chins are so variable. So instead of always trying to make lips match the nose and the chin, I simply make sure that the top lip projects two millimeters above the low lip and the other proportions are in alignment. And this tends to be a better guide rather than trying to make lips project too much. You will over project many lips if

you use lip and chin as your single guide. So look at the lips more in isolation. We should also then evaluate the volume of the lips. So volume is more about shape than it is about proportion. Don't confuse the two. Volume means that the lips are round and full, yet they still have all the details, which is the next part of our assessment. Details of the lip need to be enhanced and defended. They're often lost in overtreated lips. We want to see where the filtrum meets the villain border on the GK point as the upper lip defining point or the GK point stands for glo. He was a surgeon who named after himself. And then on the lower lip we have a very similar akin structure which basically runs if you follow the path down the filtrum to the angle of the chin typically the lower lip angle is on here. This is another important detail. Ideally the lower lip angle should be lower than the lateral part of the mouth. Obviously you can imagine as you get older this is a battle that we're always trying to win but it gets harder over time. So we're always trying to make the lower lip angle the lowest part and make this higher if we can.

Other details are the tubicle themselves. We have a lateral lip tubicle on the top and the bottom and sometimes a central tubicle. Whatever details are there, we should preserve or enhance them and try not to lose any details. You can also assess the surface of the lip for any fine lines or details that maybe you would like to soften or remove because removing little tiny creases just makes the lip more vivid in the mind's eye. So angles of the lip are the next thing to assess. We're obviously trying with these details when you enhance the lower lip angle and the tubicle, you're ultimately changing the angles. So we want dominance in the middle pointing and narrowing tapering off nicely towards the end. It's okay if it curves outwards a little bit but as soon as you curve too much laterally you start to lose the angle. So angles need to be defended and the what normally kills your angles is too much volume. Lip volume you can tell when volume has been lost because firstly lips look smaller the older you get. So they simply just involute. They start to turn inwards. The next thing that goes with that is you'll see some tiny little fine lines and wrinkles on the surface of the

lip and sometimes a few on the tops on the top of the lip and not always associated with smoking which many people find offensive if you mention it to them. It's just simply volume loss that allows a wrinkle to form. When assessing lips, remember to assess them in the context of the rest of the face. So if you don't consider what the chin and the nose and the cheeks are doing, you can easily make lips stand out in a bad way. So they start to overp project and b break some of the natural boundaries of the face. We ideally want them to meet a line that runs between the chin and the nose. But as I said, I wouldn't always try and meet that because it depends on what the nose and the chin are doing. And we also want them to be in the ratio, the golden ratio to the nose and the lip, but also the cheeks in the inverse pattern. So that if you take this the width of your lips and do the golden ratio laterally, it should actually fill to the level of the cheeks. uh you can assess the lips in motion as well and it tells you more about the dynamics with the muscle underneath. So if you have muscles that are too dominant usually pulling down then you might see sadness or negative

expression while someone is talking that can often be neutralized with botulam toxin. You can also treat the orvicularis orus if that is overactive. You'll see lips that purse too much and they look smaller than they should be. Remember small lips are a sign of anger or stress. Treating lips makes the female face look more relaxed. Same with the male face to be honest, but it's more beautiful to see a relaxed, wider, more feminine mouth. And that is what changes as you lose volume. When you have a structured assessment when assessing lips, you and your patient are more quickly on the same page with exactly what the problem is and therefore what the solution is. Understanding what the aesthetic goal is and your starting point really is what creates your ability to create a treatment plan that is actually going to solve the problem. If you are just adding volume to all your patients, you will miss opportunities to do either restoration or beautifification that's more tailored to them. Okay. So in this case, the patient has actually really lovely shaped lips. This is a beautiful cupid's bow. It is still projecting more

dominantly on the top than the bottom. So we have top lip projection, but only by about a millimeter. So a little bit more would be fine. We also have a little bit of volume loss, probably age related and also because the patient has lost weight. So, if we could show a little bit more pink, that's going to increase the proportion of the lips relative to the chin. And there's plenty of room for that because this is nowhere near the golden ratio, which is a useful guide here. So, we can make the lips slightly higher, increase the vertical height. We can also make the lip slightly wider would be fine. Her eyes are strong, but we the biggest risk factor would be to lose these details. She actually has quite delicate shapes around the filtrical columns and the tubicles. And I need to inject in such a way as I defend those angles and those details while also increasing a little bit of lip volume. Yes. So the goal here is restoration. It's not an augmentation as such. We're not trying to create new lips that will surprise the people that she knows. We're trying to actually make them essentially slip in unnoticed that she somehow looks

better but no one knows why. That's actually a I in my experience is a more common type of presentation for the average certainly patients who are in the restoration category. So it's it women 35 to 45 most often want to look good without anyone knowing why rather than stand out that can happen later but this is more common. So you need to use a product that doesn't attract moisture that holds its shape over time and using smaller amounts of it. So I wouldn't typically go above 08 of a mill. Sometimes depends on the lips, but you want to be lower volumes with a product that doesn't stand like project too much. So I like Juven lift. There are other products that are similar. So it's not I'm not representing the company, but I am I found over time that holds its shape well and doesn't attract more moisture like many of the products which seem to get bigger when you see the patient in 4 months time. Okay. So this is Juvéom Volt with a 31 gauge needle. It's how it comes in the packet. I also have a KA ring uh control ring on the back which I find makes aspirating a lot more efficient, easier, and means I

don't move around as I'm aspirating. Highly recommend them. There are many other products you can use, but this is the best control ring I found. What am I thinking at this point? Remember in the assessment I noted that she has quite delicate structures. So, this is not a lip that I want to be I never am, but I want to be thinking in as much resolution as possible about exactly where this product goes. If I inject on the Vminin border here, I'm actually going to make her look firstly, it's going to cause migration over time, but it's also going to inflate the white part of the lip and the pink next to each other. And really, what I want is the opposite. I want the pink to out compete the white, which means I need to be well within the pink lip and also superior to the level of the muscle and the artery. You've got between 3 and 5 mm before you're close to the artery, which is actually quite a long way. If I wanted to get to it, I' I'd look significantly deeper than I will do when I actually do this. It's another good thing about this product is it allows you to be more superficial. I'm going to line the needle up with the exact

vermillion border, which is not where I'm going to inject. I'm now going to go about 2 millimeters lower than that, maybe one and a half millimeters. And I'm aiming for the first defining point, which is the GK point. This is the projection of the cupid's bow. So, I'm just going in. I'm trying to keep my depth as superficial as I can the whole way because this product is great superficially. The artery is underneath and I'm also making sure I'm not in the white part of the lip because I think that is responsible for most of the migration that you see. When I elevate this needle, it elevates mucosa, not dermis. Okay, I'm now ready to inject. I usually like to do a little bolus at the GK point. So, you should see that project a little bit. Then on the way out, I'll do a linear thread. I don't come all the way out because I want to add a bit more volume to the lip

itself. So I'm now in the tubicle and I'm doing a little bit after an aspiration. 0.05. Then I'll do a little squeeze just to make sure it's in the right plane. So what I like about that method is especially if your patient is a bit sensitive is I've done essentially three injections in one. There will be less swelling as a result. Let me point out something else because there's been some weight loss. You can see a bit more of the anatomy underneath and there are obviously nasal labial fold. There's the just above here you see a little bit of emptiness where there are tiny little creases and you can see a natural weak point a fracture line that forms basically where oricularis orus is no longer attached to the surface of the skin. It's actually directly attached to the skin here and then it loses its attachment and there's a natural weak point. I need to make sure that I defend against this line getting worse because if I've just strengthened this and I

don't do anything about here, we will might see her back in a week or two weeks time with a new crease. So, I can do that often with one injection point as well. So, I'm going to go in just break border. I'm under the pink again. Once again, I can do a linear thread. Can then change angle. Do a little bit in the lip body. Same again. 0.05 ms. Now I can change angle and go. Now remember, I'm going through the insertion point of the muscle, but I'm trying to be in dermis here. I need to be as superficial as possible, which is why I'm using my supporting finger. Little bit of it. Almost got too superficial there. Could see a gray of the needle appearing. So, I'm now above the lip slightly for an unusual injection for me, which is just right where you might worry about

migration, but I'm only putting a tiny drop, and it's to strengthen this. So, that's now less likely to bend. So, it's a defensive injection. I'm going to change the needle. I like sharp needles. Top lip to bottom ratio is now too strong. So if she saw herself now, she would just see this big top lip. So we need to balance that out with the bottom lip. And so it's very similar in concept. Defend the angles, add volume to the tubicles, uh treat laterally with definition and volume in mind. uh and try and do it in as little trauma as possible. So entry point is above that insertion point where the muscle is attached to the both the dermis and the mucosa edging in checking my depth all the way along on the lower lip angle. Aspirate

can inject. Now this is doing an interesting thing you sometimes see with lips. pulls the tissue in. Thought to be down to little connective tissue strands. It looks like you're injecting and removing volume, which is strange, but we will massage that in a second. So, now I'm in the tubicle. That's an interesting little indentation. That's basically muscle connecting to the surface of the lip. Little tiny strands of connective tissue. And if you get them on one side or the other, it pulls or pushes the tube, the strands, and then pulls the surface of the lip in. And as soon as you squeeze it like this, it will squeeze through those little strands and get rid of that. So don't freak out when you see it. It's normal. Yeah. >> Oh my god. Wow. Looks amazing. >> Yep. If you use this framework and

systematically assess each patient this way, you will be confident in the outcome you're going to get because it is so directly connected with the technique and the aesthetic potential for each patient. One of the hardest things with lips, particularly with patients who are afraid of overt treatment, is swelling. Swelling is a normal part of the process, but it's also highly variable. Some patients swell enormously and others hardly swell at all. A nervous patient who doesn't want to be overtreated, who also swells, can find the mirror moment rather anxietyprovoking. And often the clinician who's standing just behind them feels exactly the same way. The key to this is to talk about it before the procedure, during procedure, and just before you show them the mirror. They need to know that swelling is a normal part of the process and it will settle over time and not to assess the end result at that moment. Having a systematic framework that you associate with techniques and volumes over time will give you confidence that even if the patient's lips have swelled a lot, you do not need to fear and they will look natural once time has passed.
